summary: The premium version of the legendary Evo built with Penta TX canopy material. Lighter weight translates to better low-end, quicker response, and an even wider wind range than the standard Evo.

sources.summary: Considered the gold standard for all-around kites. The Penta TX material noticeably improves low-end power and response.
Premium price is justified according to most riders. The weight savings from Penta TX are immediately noticeable.
Called the best all-around kite money can buy, with improved drift for wave riding and better upwind performance.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Review 15 - Rebel SLS & D/Lab VS Evo D/Lab
reviews.excerpt: hello and welcome to yet another plus-sized kite review where this time around I will be focusing on the top Big Air kites from industry Giant duone in particular the 24 and 25 Rebel SLS the new Rebel dlab and my experience with my own Evo dlabs and how they compare if you are a fan of my reviews and would like to contribute to me being able to fly
reviews.verdict: ter size next we are going to move on to the rebel daab but because of the length of this video at this stage what I'm going to do is post this as a part one and we will carry on with the rebel DB in a part two falling right behind this one so as always please like And subscribe throw any comments on you have about my experiences with evos or your experiences with them how you feel about your Rebels and all that and I'll see you in the very next video for part two where we go into the rebel dlab
reviews.full_transcript: hello and welcome to yet another plus-sized kite review where this time around I will be focusing on the top Big Air kites from industry Giant duone in particular the 24 and 25 Rebel SLS the new Rebel dlab and my experience with my own Evo dlabs and how they compare if you are a fan of my reviews and would like to contribute to me being able to fly around New Zealand to chase wind and be able to keep making them with some regularity I have set up an online page for the option of tipping this is only to help me recoup some of the costs this year of making these reviews and it is of course totally optional but very much appreciated naturally I will be keeping these Reviews free on YouTube no matter what for this review I will start with discussing my experiences for the last year and a half on my current quiver the 2023 Evo dlabs going over the pros and pawns the ups and downs and how they still stack up after all of the other kites I've tried since because I've had so many questions sent to me about these kites in the last year or so I'll do my best to include information that seems to come up more often but as always if I left anything else please hit me up in the comments let's start with a quick uh thanks to Mac kiteboarding as usual for getting these Rebels out to me uh uh this year and you will see the haime board they sent me for the Board review as well in a lot of the footage since my Board review I should update that I have held on to this one to continue reviewing duone gear and I also have been finding that this bigger board size the 139 has been very handy for the lower to Mid strength winds where my Atmos carbon 138 just wouldn't uh stand up I've been slowly getting the hang of rioting it since the review and although I still find it a bit challenging at times to keep my control uh as the wind creeps up and I don't tend to jump as vert vertically with it I do find it uh is getting is growing on me and it seems to ride a fair bit larger than my Atmos so getting up wind in lighter winds has been great with it if you have not seen my original review of the SLS 2023 and and the Evo dlab that led me after only one season to buy my first Evo in my current quiver then I highly recommend starting there as I will no doubt be referencing it a lot so I'll put a link to that video in the description given these Lula Evo laabs were by far the most expensive kites I've ever owned I thought it would be funny to work out how much they cost me per hour for use thus far and to better explain why when I get asked all the time what kites I will be buying next my general answer is is I'm probably going to need at least another year on these dlabs to get my money's worth my current personal quiver is still an 8 102 and they have all seen at least 50 more hours on each of them with my 10 alone having seen over 130 hours of ride time since I bought it so it is certainly being the best value of the bunch costing me to date around $26 per hour of use excluding the cost of the bars of course the 12 is also still a pretty good deal so far at $36 per hour my 8 m sadly despite a month-long trip to Cape Town last year is still sitting at over $130 per hour of use so far but that was certainly the most enjoyable time on the water needless to say in terms of their durability and wear each kite has held up quite well but I do take very good care of them of course I never roll them up after a session but instead use larger softly folded panel like folds when packing up and I never try to inflate them without first manually unfolding them if you didn't know rolling up alula or inflating a folded alula kite isn't the friendliest move for your bladder given how stiff the Ula material is one significant fault with this model has been that they all need a bladder replacement within the first year due to them getting micro holes that caused very slow leaks which was due to the original versions of the bladder being too thin or perhaps lower quality I believe duone has since replaced the bulk of these on the market as far as I'm aware and duone were kind enough to send me free replacements for them and newer ones that were more robust each weighing a little bit more approximately 100 G for the eight meter for example annoyingly because I am down in New Zealand it did take a few months for them to get here and I had to pay a local kite shop to install them as I was too chicken to try it myself on a couple of the kites I've needed to get pairs done to the deflate valve on the wing tips because I found out after the second one going that the bridal can actually get caught on it when you self-launch or are not paying very close attention to it when another person launches you as they flip the kite and it gets c on it as seen here I don't think this is a fault of the quality of the manufacturing but perhaps a poor design that it's even possible to happen like that either way I glued them all shut and put zip ties over it so it won't happen again and they have been fine ever since there is also some noticeable wear along the Ula material itself unfortunately and the fabric is starting to split a little bit on a couple of my kites I have seen some even worse wear on Friends kites as you can see in this image here so the few of us who did buy evos around the same time last year are starting to worry when that alula is going to start being a problem other than this my kites have held up really well and I've really just had my main issues with the trust bars themselves as you know from my other videos or perhaps my Instagram a few of us who got them did have issues with line snapping so have we have since replaced all our duone lines with Lin Smith lines and had no issues with them since duone have since done a recall on the lines on that 2024 bar so the 2025 might not have the same issues but why take the chance in my opinion you may also notice that I have started to use the Harlem bar a lot lately this is particularly because I like the bar and its design as I point out in the Harlem Thrive review but also because I have had a really hard time getting this particular replacement part for my trust bars and I'm also admittedly starting to get a bit more nervous about using them after a year and a half at the start of the Year while I was chasing up the bladder Replacements I was in contact with several people from boards and More in an email chain about buying a whole lot of these to go with the bladders and to their credit eventually they sent me two of them for free with my bladders but when I kept requesting more of them and offering to pay for them they just sto replying to my emails and I've not been able to Source any more since so for this reason I would not suggest probably buying from Duo te directly and take a little Caution because they are probably just too big a company to be able to deal direct with individuals so unless you have a really good retail store that you have access to you might have some issues in my experience all the retail stores I've dealt with who sell Duo te like mac kiteboarding of course have always been amazing to deal with and going direct to duone is obviously just a bit more of a struggle so at least if you're like me and are far away in a small Market of course so somewhat self-inflicted bladder or line fault related issues aside my kites are still going well and fingers cross they last another year in terms of their performance well if you've seen my other reviews you'll know that in my opinion they have so far remained The Benchmark for overall performance kite that no other model I've tried has really come quite so close to on the whole when you also factor in things like their wind range and versatility for other disciplines sure there are other kites that now are easier to use particularly for Big Air in the smaller sizes or are easier to progress with but when you factor in everything in terms of potential performance from boosting potential to hangtime to low-end power multidiscipline versatility all with some of the lightest bar pressure I still think the Evo dlab has been the topof the pile of all the kites I've been trying the ability to ride a kite size down from your mates if you are the same size or like me happen to weigh 15 to 20 kgs more most of the time but can still ride the same size kite as them and always have the same low-end and max power is just such a blessing their huge wind range as well has been great as they do really account for almost two whole kite sizes so if you look at my 8102 quiver I'm very rarely feeling like I needed uh 9 or 11 meter in the time that I've had them in terms of top end they're of course so light and responsive that although quite challenging to get the hang of it first once you have I found the 8 m allows me to be on the water for much longer uh sessions in the really nuking winds without tiring as quickly and the potential power and lift they give me are still unlike anything else I've felt in any other kits that I've tested to the point that I am fairly confident my only two ever jumps over 30 m so far on woo would have been unlikely to have happened on any other kite it is definitely worth considering that of course I do ride these kites the most so I am the most familiar with them now more so obviously than any of the other kites I'm testing but I am still fairly confident that they offer the most of any kite out there so far since their release now with that said there are certainly not the most recent kites to hit the market from duone so let's get into these very I Rebels that have joined the range since the release if you have not seen my last video as I mentioned it is worth checking it out as most of my general feelings about the rebel SLS in particular are the same and I still really enjoy riding each of these kites at least in the 9 M sizes I have tested they're just so super friendly for boosting and offer perhaps the most easily accessible power on the market for all levels of Rider now let's start the comparison updating the rebel SLS models with the 2024 and 2025 because the last time I'd rode a rebel was the amazing 2023 model from the previous review and I covered that a year or so ago I did not have access to the 2023 model when I rode these kites so it is a bit hard for me to give a very accurate account of how the kites changed from those years but the first thing I felt when I tried the 24 model was that my idea of lighter to medium bar pressure had certainly shifted after a year on the dlabs and other kites that I'd been testing particularly modern alula and Harlem kites I found the 2024 certainly felt heavier than I remembered the 2023 model being however at the time I was also comparing that kite to my orbits and my core XR 7s that I would ride a lot when the wind was 30 knots or more I wouldn't say it was crazy heavy just more so than I remembered the 2023 model being so around the medium to light heavy in terms of overall pressure in terms of the general performance other than that it felt very much the same as the 2023 but perhaps just a little grunt here but not anything too significant for me to say for sure there was a dramatic Improvement particularly given I had ridden them a year apart going from the 2024 to the 2025 again not a huge difference when you are comparing them to all the other Kites on the market right now with newer fabrics and various design advancements but I'd say enough for an enthusiastic consistent Rebel Rider to find some differences and potential advantages the 2025 does feel a little bit friendlier in general from being slightly lighter on the bar and I would say a little bit more direct and quicker through the loops perhaps it was the shifting conditions of course I was riding in but it might not as well be quite as grunty as the 2024 so it might have shaved off a touch of potential power for ease of use and looping potential this is of course just a little bit of guesswork as I was jumping similar Heights on all the rebels I tested in the respective winds I was testing them with both of these models there isn't too much I would say differently to that last review on the rebel I really enjoy how easy these kites are to just jump on and send big with as they have perhaps the largest sweet spot for jumping of any kite on the market there is still a little noticeable delay when engaging the bar compared to say my evos or other pop Kites on the market that don't have pulley but I found I did get used to it fairly quickly and I even actually went out and tested the 2025 model with 24 M lines as you can see in this clip and found it also surprisingly easy to get comfortable with with this honking megal Loop being only the third jump of a fairly lit and very Gusty session the kite specialty is certainly going big and easily with good stability in the kite and keeping you up there with some of the best hang time you can find particularly for dakron kites I'd have to say they would probably be the best of the kites I've tried in terms of lift and hang time for dakron with probably the code V2 coming in just behind them for hangtime and perhaps the fly Surfer era just behind them for boosting potential like in my previous review I don't think these are the most ideal kites in say sizes N9 and up to get into for your first kite Loops but anyone with some experience looping should find them pretty easy to get on and fire some big ones comfortably the kite certainly Loops well enough though as do most kites these days really but they of course tend to go a bit lower in the window have more pull on them and you can put them in the wrong position through the loop that can make them hesitant to climb back up at times this can also happen in less consistent Gusty winds I found as well but in general if you are jumping with good form not over sending the kite before you Loop so it goes too far out of the window or sheing out too much through the loop trying to get the kite lower then you should be fine and have no worry about this happening too much if at all because there tend to require a little more accurate piloting through the loops they can be a bit nerve inducing for trying rotations on because the kites are a little harder to track as well and they don't have as direct a feel sometimes as some of the other kites I've mentioned they also tend to loop with a bit more power than some of the other top dakron Loopers like orbits or Nitros for example once again I'd love to have a go riding any of these rebels in sizes seven or eight in really strong 40 plus winds because I think they would just be amazingly fun in those conditions and I've never tried them in the larger sizes 10 and up I'd wager they are still great fun to float around and do board offs Etc but they probably wouldn't be a pick for larger size looping Kites and although the lowend is probably good for dakron 5 strut they wouldn't come close to my alula evos or say some of the other kites like the Harlem thrives that I I also tested in a 12 meter size next we are going to move on to the rebel daab but because of the length of this video at this stage what I'm going to do is post this as a part one and we will carry on with the rebel DB in a part two falling right behind this one so as always please like And subscribe throw any comments on you have about my experiences with evos or your experiences with them how you feel about your Rebels and all that and I'll see you in the very next video for part two where we go into the rebel dlab

structured_review.summary: The Evo D/Lab (and by extension the SLS platform) represents Jason Montreal's personal benchmark for all-around performance after 1.5+ years and 130+ hours on his own kites. Its Aluula construction delivers an exceptional wind range, outstanding low-end power, and big-air potential that consistently outperforms heavier dakron rivals. The main caveats are real durability concerns with early bladders and canopy wear, plus the premium price demanding serious commitment.
pros: Massive wind range — effectively covers two kite sizes
Industry-leading low-end power for its size
Light bar pressure with highly responsive, direct feel
Exceptional boosting potential and hang time
Versatile across disciplines — from freeride to big air
Durable overall once bladder issues are resolved
cons: Early bladders developed micro-holes requiring replacement within first year
Aluula canopy fabric showing material splitting after heavy use
Deflate valve design can snag bridle during self-launch
High price demands long ownership to justify cost per session
Duotone direct customer support reportedly slow and inconsistent for remote buyers
structured_review.best_for: Experienced freeriders and big-air riders who want maximum wind range, light bar feel, and top-tier boosting performance in a single premium quiver.
structured_review.not_for: Beginners or budget-conscious riders unwilling to manage premium material care requirements and potential early maintenance issues.
structured_review.rec_blurb: Jason's personal benchmark after 130+ hours — the kite nothing else has quite beaten.
